subject: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
concepts: Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues — Definition: A nonzero vector x is an eigenvector of a square matrix A if Ax = λx for some scalar λ (the eigenvalue). Ax points in exactly the same (or exactly opposite) direction as x; λ measures by how much A stretches or flips x. In plain terms, eigenvectors are the special directions that a matrix does not rotate — it only scales them.

Eigenvalue λ = 0 and Singular Matrices: If a matrix A is singular, then Ax = 0 for some nonzero x, making λ = 0 an eigenvalue and x a vector in the null space. In other words, a matrix has λ = 0 as an eigenvalue if and only if it is singular.

Finding Eigenvalues via the Characteristic Equation: To avoid the difficulty that both λ and x are unknown in Ax = λx, rewrite it as (A − λI)x = 0. For a nonzero x to exist, the matrix A − λI must be singular, so its determinant must be zero: det(A − λI) = 0. This equation (the characteristic equation) is a polynomial of degree n in λ, whose n roots are the n eigenvalues. Find eigenvalues first, then find eigenvectors.

Finding Eigenvectors After the Eigenvalues: Once an eigenvalue λ is known, the corresponding eigenvectors are all nonzero vectors in the null space of (A − λI). This is done by standard elimination on (A − λI) and finding a basis for its null space — a procedure already mastered in the course.

Trace = Sum of Eigenvalues, Determinant = Product of Eigenvalues: For an n×n matrix A, the sum of all eigenvalues equals the trace (sum of diagonal entries: a₁₁ + a₂₂ + … + a_{nn}), and the product of all eigenvalues equals the determinant. For a 2×2 matrix this means the characteristic polynomial is λ² − trace·λ + det = 0.

Shifting a Matrix by cI Shifts Eigenvalues by c, Leaves Eigenvectors Unchanged: If Ax = λx, then (A + cI)x = (λ + c)x. Adding a scalar multiple of the identity shifts every eigenvalue by c but does not change any eigenvector. This does NOT generalise to adding an arbitrary matrix B.

Eigenvalues of A + B Are NOT Generally the Sum of Eigenvalues: Knowing eigenvalues of A and eigenvalues of B separately tells you nothing about the eigenvalues of A + B, because A and B typically have different eigenvectors. The eigenvector of A is generally not an eigenvector of B.

Symmetric Matrices Have Real Eigenvalues; Anti-Symmetric Matrices Have Purely Imaginary Eigenvalues: A real symmetric matrix (A^T = A) always produces real eigenvalues. A real anti-symmetric matrix (A^T = −A) produces purely imaginary eigenvalues. General real matrices lie between these extremes and can have complex eigenvalues appearing in conjugate pairs.

Triangular Matrices: Eigenvalues Are the Diagonal Entries: For a triangular matrix, det(A − λI) is the product of the diagonal entries (d₁−λ)(d₂−λ)…, so the eigenvalues are exactly the diagonal entries. This makes eigenvalue computation immediate for triangular matrices.

Repeated Eigenvalues May Cause a Shortage of Eigenvectors: If an eigenvalue λ is repeated with algebraic multiplicity k, the geometric multiplicity (dimension of the null space of A − λI) may be less than k. A matrix can then fail to have n independent eigenvectors — it cannot be fully diagonalized. This is the 'degenerate' or defective case.
examples: Projection matrix P onto a plane in R^3: vectors x lying in the plane satisfy Px = x, so they are eigenvectors with eigenvalue λ = 1. Vectors x perpendicular to the plane satisfy Px = 0, so they are eigenvectors with eigenvalue λ = 0. The two eigenvalues of P are 1 and 0.

Permutation (swap) matrix A = [[0,1],[1,0]]: eigenvector x = [1,1]^T gives Ax = [1,1]^T = 1·x (λ = 1); eigenvector x = [−1,1]^T gives Ax = [1,−1]^T = −1·[−1,1]^T (λ = −1). Trace = 0 confirms λ₁ + λ₂ = 1 + (−1) = 0.

Symmetric matrix A = [[3,1],[1,3]]: characteristic polynomial is det(A − λI) = (3−λ)² − 1 = λ² − 6λ + 8 = (λ−4)(λ− 2) = 0, giving λ₁ = 4 and λ₂ = 2. Trace = 6 = 4+2 ✓; det = 8 = 4×2 ✓. Eigenvector for λ=4: null space of A−4I = [[−1,1],[1,−1]] is x₁ = [1,1]^T. Eigenvector for λ=2: null space of A−2I = [[1,1],[1,1]] is x₂ = [−1,1]^T. Note x₁·x₂ = 0: they are perpendicular.

Connecting the symmetric matrix [[3,1],[1,3]] to the permutation matrix [[0,1],[1,0]] via a shift: [[3,1],[1,3]] = [[0,1],[1,0]] + 3I, so eigenvalues shift from {1, −1} to {4, 2}, while eigenvectors [1,1]^T and [−1,1]^T remain unchanged.

90° rotation matrix Q = [[0,−1],[1,0]]: trace = 0, det = 1, characteristic equation λ² + 1 = 0. Eigenvalues are λ = i and λ = −i (complex conjugate pair), because no real vector can remain parallel to itself after a 90° rotation. Q is anti-symmetric (Q^T = −Q), consistent with purely imaginary eigenvalues.

Defective triangular matrix A = [[3,1],[0,3]]: characteristic polynomial (3−λ)² = 0 gives a repeated eigenvalue λ = 3. Null space of A−3I = [[0,1],[0,0]] is one-dimensional: x₁ = [1,0]^T. There is no second independent eigenvector, so A is not diagonalizable.
analogy: A matrix acting on vectors is compared to a function in calculus: 'In goes a number x, out comes f(x)' becomes 'In goes a vector x, out comes Ax.' Eigenvectors are the special inputs for which the output points in the same direction as the input — analogous to fixed points or scaling inputs of a function.

The characteristic equation det(A − λI) = 0 is motivated by the familiar idea that a singular matrix has a null space: 'We can't use elimination because λ and x are both unknown, so we first force the matrix to be singular (to guarantee a nonzero solution x) by setting its determinant to zero, then find x by elimination.'

Symmetric vs. anti-symmetric matrices are described as opposite extremes on a spectrum: symmetric matrices (A^T = A) are 'as close to diagonal as possible' and have real eigenvalues with perpendicular eigenvectors, while anti-symmetric matrices (A^T = −A) are 'as far from symmetric as possible' and have purely imaginary eigenvalues. General real matrices lie between these extremes.
reviewed_transcript: What if a matrix didn't rotate a vector at all — just stretched or shrunk it? That's the magic of eigenvectors. Every square matrix has special "natural directions" — vectors it acts on like a simple zoom lens, scaling them without tilting them. That scaling factor is the eigenvalue. Take a projection matrix that flattens everything onto a single line: vectors already on that line stay put, eigenvalue one — no change. Vectors pointing away get crushed to zero, eigenvalue zero — complete collapse. Or flip to a permutation matrix that swaps two coordinates: the diagonal direction just bounces back unchanged, eigenvalue one, while the off-diagonal direction reverses, eigenvalue negative one. To hunt these down, you solve one elegant equation — det(A minus λI) equals zero — and the trace and determinant of A give you instant sanity checks on your eigenvalues. Master eigenvectors and you hold the skeleton key to everything from Google's PageRank to quantum mechanics.
